findings.description: load() re-runs a full os.path.exists stat + file open + full read + yaml.load parse + complete os.environ scan on EVERY call with zero caching, so config (static data) is recomputed from scratch each time it is accessed.

get(), require(), and feature_enabled() each call self.load(), so what look like O(1) accessors (especially a per-request feature-flag check) actually trigger a disk stat+open+read and a full YAML parse per invocation, scaling latency/CPU with file size on every call site hit.

feature_enabled() fully reloads and re-parses the entire config file just to read one nested key from feature_flags; called per-request or in a loop this turns a cheap boolean check into repeated disk I/O and YAML parsing.

os.path.exists() (one stat) followed by open() (another stat) is a redundant double syscall; a try/except FileNotFoundError around open() avoids the extra stat, which is amplified by the per-call reload pattern.

yaml.load(f) is called without SafeLoader (deferred to critic-security: arbitrary-code-execution risk); noted here only because PyYAML's parse is the dominant CPU cost of the per-call reload, not as a perf finding.

Inverted precedence vs docstring (deferred to critic-logic): _read_env() is applied before _read_file(), so update() lets file values override env vars, contradicting the documented 'env > file' precedence.
